claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me quickly review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, **do you have any children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you please tell me how many children you'd like to check eligibility for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm interested in claiming for. His name is Alex, and he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions:

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. That's helpful to know.

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Now let me ask:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

Just one more question to complete the assessment:

**Is Alex currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or are there any special circumstances regarding where he stays?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - Alex has been in hospital for a while now. He's been there for about 11 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. That's an important detail.

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital?** This could include things like buying him clothes, toys, treats, or other items for his use.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I still buy his clothes, magazines, and his favorite food while he's in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit based on what you've told me.

Based on the official rules I've reviewed, here's my assessment:

**You ARE eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: According to the rules, Child Benefit payments might be affected if a child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. Since Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, he is still within the 12-week threshold. Additionally, even after 12 weeks, there are exceptions - one being if you regularly spend money on the child while they're in hospital, which you are doing by buying Alex clothes, magazines, and his favorite food.

You meet all the key eligibility criteria:
- Alex is under 16 years old (he's 8)
- You live in the UK
- You're responsible for Alex and he normally lives with you
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- His hospital stay is currently within the allowable timeframe

**Important note:** If Alex's hospital stay extends beyond 12 weeks, you should contact HMRC to inform them, but you may still remain eligible given that you're regularly spending money on him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your eligibility?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit. He is 8 years old (under 16), the claimant lives in the UK, Alex normally lives with the claimant, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is still within the 12-week threshold before Child Benefit is typically affected. Furthermore, even if the hospital stay extends beyond 12 weeks, the claimant is regularly spending money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and favorite food), which is an exception that allows Child Benefit to continue. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. All standard eligibility criteria are met: the child is under 16, the claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for the child who normally lives with them, and no one else is claiming. Although Alex has been in hospital for 11 weeks, this is within the 12-week limit, and the claimant regularly spends money on him which would allow eligibility to continue even beyond 12 weeks.
